text: ABU'L-ABBAS, or Ba'LIBA's, a large v. of Luristán, on the Abi-Zard, where it descends from the Mungasht mountains into the plain of Baghi-Malik.
ABU'-LAHOR, a town of Romelia, situated in the midst of a fertile district, 36 m. NNW of Lepanto.
ABU'L-CASIM, a fortified town in the pashalik of Baghdad, on the l. bank of the Euphrates, 22 m. NNW of Hillah.

ABU-'L-HOFRAH, or Bu Hofrah, [Father-pit, AR.], a well in the province of Shawiyah, in Marocco, 60 m. E of Mazighan, near the site of a Mauritanian or Roman station, and of some ruins called Caria. Lat. 33° 10' N, long. 7° 8' W.— Washington in Lond. Geog. Journ., vol. i., p. 152.

ABU'LL, or ABU'-ALI, a river of Syria, also called the Kadisha, or Nahr Kavdas, which, descending from the Libanus range, in the district of El Kura, flows through a beautiful and picturesque valley, and after traversing the town of Tripoli, discharges itself into the sea to the SSE of Point Marina. It is a shallow rapid stream, and unnavigable even by boats.

ABULLIONTE, a small town on an island at the NE angle of a lake of the same name, 17 m. W of Brusah in Asia Minor, on the site of the ancient Apollonia ad Rhyndacum. The lake is 16 m. long from E to W, by 8 m. broad; and contains several islands. The river Rhyndacus, entering the lake on its SW, and leaving it at its NW angle, falls into the sea of Marmora.—Rawlinson.
ABULLO, a town on the E coast of the isle of Leyte, one of the Philippines, in N lat. 10° 40', E long 125°.

ABU'-MANNEH, or ABU'-Mana, a town of Upper Egypt, near the r. bank of the Nile, 25 m. E of Ghenneh. On the 3d of March 1799, a division of the French army, under General Friant, defeated a large body of Mamluks and Arabs near this place.
ABUMBA, a town of Africa, on the Melinda coast, near Brava.
ABU'-MED (Ras), a low point on the E coast of the Red sea, in N lat. 24° 50'. The coast is here fronted by low sandy islets and reefs.

ABU'-MANDUR, a place of fashionable resort NW of Dahalak I. Lat. 16° 5' N.—E. I. C. Chart. on the Nile, about 2 m. from Rosetta. It commands an extensive view both up and down the stream. on the l. bank of the Damietta branch of the Nile, on ABU Denon supposes this place may mark the site of the ancient Bolbitinum.

ABU'-MOHAMMED (Ras), a cape in the Arabian gulf, forming the S point of the peninsula of Sinai, and dividing the gulfs of Suez and Akabah. The coast about it is low and rugged; and it extends in a low tongue of land, 3 leagues to the S of the high land which forms behind it. N lat. 27° 43'.— Wellsted and Burckhardt.
ABUNA, a river of Peru, flowing by a NE course into the Rio Madeira, under S lat. 9° 30'.
ABU'R, a mountain of Arabia, in the prov. of Yemen, 21 m. WSW of Kataba.
ABURAMLA, a native name of the country of Ginjar, to the W of Abyssinia.
ABU'-RO'ASH, a ruined pyramid, about 6 m. N of the pyramids of Gizeh. It stands on a ridge of hills which skirt the desert behind Kerdásseh.
ABURRA (SAN BARTOLEME DE), a town of Colombia, in the prov. and gov. of Antioquia, founded in 1542, in a fertile and extensive valley.

ABURY, or AVEBURY, a v. in the county of Wilts, celebrated for a stupendous assemblage of stones of great antiquity, artificially arranged. The main body of this monument, whether religious or sepulchral, appears to have originally consisted of one vast circle of 100 enormous stones, each from 15 to 17 ft. in height, and nearly as much in breadth; the distance between them being about 27 ft. The whole circlewhich was at least 1,300 ft. in diameter—was environed by a deep ditch, 80 ft. wide and very deep, with a lofty rampart, 60 ft. broad enclosing the whole. Within the great circle, were two smaller double or concentric circles, by the side of each other, also formed of huge stones, and resembling the first in size and arrangement. In the centre of the one pair stood a pillar 20 ft. high; and in that of the other, a cromlech. Long avenues of rude large stones extended the distance of a mile to other circles. This gigantic relic is now nearly destroyed, much of it having been broken up to build the village of A. itself. But at the beginning of last century 650 stones remained. A. is distant 6 m. from Marlborough, and 81 from London.
ABU'-SAID, a town of Kurdistan, on the l. bank of the Tigris, 55 m. NE of Sinjar.—Also a v. in the Great Oasis, a little to the NE of Kasr-el-Hajar.

ABU'-SAMA, a town of Abyssinia, to the SE of Adowa. ABU'-SAMBUL, ABU'-SIMBEL [Irby and Mangles], or IBSAMBUL [Burckhardt], a place in Lower Nubia, remarkable for its two rock-cut temples, and beautiful colossi, supposed to be of the time of Rameses the Great, which were first explored by Belzoni. They are excavated in the rock, in the face of the mountain-cliff: the lesser about 35, and the greater about 50, ft. above the level of the river. A. is in N lat. 22° 2′; 26 m. N of the cataracts of Wadi-Halfa; nearly opposite the extremity of the range of the Mockatan mountains, which here present the appearance of a number of isolated pyramidal hills rising up from the sand like a gigantic camp; their colour, alternate patches of red and black like a heap of recent ashes. Directly opposite the temples, there is a large cultivated island not marked in the maps. -Irby and Mangles. -St. John.
ABU'-SEIELAT, a town on the frontiers of Lower Nubia, 8 m. SE of Assouan.
ABU'-SENUM, a town of Sudan, in the kingdom of Bergu, 120 m. E of Wara.
ABU'-SHEHR. See BUSHIRE.
ABU'-SHERAYIR, an islet in the Red sea, 23 m.

ABU'-SIR, or ABU'-SEER, a town of Lower Egypt, the site of the ancient Busiris, in N lat. 30° 57'.- [ Also a tower on the coast of Lower Egypt, 23 m. SW by W from Alexandria, known to mariners under the name of the Arab's Tower, or the first point of Egypt descried by vessels approaching Alexandria from the W.-Also a prominent rock, about 150 ft. in height [St. John], on the banks of the Nile, in Nubia, in N lat. 21° 53'; at the point where the rocky wilderness of Sukkot begins, and from the summit of which a magnificent view, comprehending the whole extent of the falls, is obtained. "Looking towards the S," says Mr. J. A. St. John, "we behold the Nileits channel being about a mile in breadth-emerging from among a chaos of rocks, as if it here sprung in all its grandeur from the earth. Flowing northward between innumerable islands of green porphyry piled into the most fantastic forms, it at length arrives at the point where the water precipitates itself, with prodigious noise and velocity, over an abrupt descent in its bed, having no certain direction, dashed now towards one side now towards the other by opposing rocks, vexed with whirlpools, and broken into eddies. In many parts it seems to be bursting through some enormous sluice,-while fall beyond fall, covered with foam and hurling aloft clouds of spray, present themselves in magnificent succession to the eye. Almost in the midst of these, protected by some jutting promontory, we discover smooth expanses of water, unruffled as a summer lake, affording a beautiful contrast with the savage uproar of the cataracts. Meanwhile, the noise of the dashing water is so loud that the whole region round appears to be shaken by continual thunder, the ears being incessantly filled with this sound, which mingles itself with the conception of what is beheld, and powerfully influences the decision of the judgment." [Travels in the Valley of the Nile, vol. i., pp. 476, 477.]—The derivation of the name, in the two latter cases, is probably from Jebel Apsir, i. e. High rock.

ABU'T, a town of Sennaar, about 20 m. W of Wadi-Medinah, and 10 E of Monakil. The land is not cultivated between Abút and Monakil; but between Abut and Medinah, which is on the Blue Nile, the surface is a dead level, and the land of the richest quality.—Holroyd. ABU"TCHA, a river of Siberia, which flows into the Sana, in N lat. 66° 30'. ABU"THUBBIE, a village of El Ahsa, in Arabia, on the Persian gulf. Lat. 24° 29' N, long. 54° 31' E. ABU'TIG, ABOOTE'EG [Wilkinson], or ABOUTIC, [Bowring's Report], a village of Upper Egypt, on the 1. bank of the Nile, in N lat. 27° 1'. It is the Abutis of Latin writers .- Wilkinson.

ABUZABEL, a town of Upper Egypt, in the district of Siout. The present pasha of Egypt established an hospital, a medical school, a botanic garden, a veterinary school, and a primary school here; but all these institutions have already been given up or removed elsewhere.
ABWERDEN, a small town of East Prussia, in the bailiwick of Natangen, 3 m. SSW of Konigsberg.
ABY, a parish in Lincolnshire. Acres 1,560. Pop. 312.

ABY DOS, a ruined city of Upper Egypt, celebrated in ancient times for the palace of Memnon, and a splendid temple dedicated to Osiris. Its modern name is Arabat-el-Matfoon [ Wilkinson], or El Arabat Madfoumah [Yates],-i. e. Arabat the buried, or Arabat beneath the Sands. It lies to the S of Girgeh, under N lat. 26° 12', about 7 m. from the l. bank of the Nile and the village of Samata. In one of the chambers of what is supposed to be the ruins of the Memnonium, Mr. W. Bankes, in 1818, discovered a large hieroglyphical tablet, which has proved to be a genealogical account of the immediate predecessors of Rameses the Great, the Sethos of Manetho, and the Sesostris of Herodotus, who ascended the throne B. C. 1473.—Champollion, Precis 244.
ABY DUS. See DARDANELLES.

ABYLA, a mountain-group on the N coast of Marocco, of which the extreme NE shoulder running out into the peninsula of Ceuta, forms one of the pillars of Hercules, as they were called by the ancients, directly opposite to Calpe-now Gibraltarin Spain, from which it is distant 18 m .- The western shoulder of the Abyla group, the Jebel Thatut, forms, outside, or to the W of the peninsula of Ceuta, a cape called Cabo Leona, or Leona Point.
ABYN, a small port of Sweden, in W Bothnia, 10 m. S of Pitea.
ABYO, one of the Philippine islands, between Mindanao and Luzon.

ABYSSINIA, or ABESSINIA, An extensive and important country on the eastern side of N Africa. Its extent and boundaries cannot be determined with precision; for geographers are not agreed as to what districts are properly comprised within it, and its political limits have varied greatly since the Portuguese first made us acquainted with it. Confining A. to the provinces actually under the government of Christian or Mahommedan princes, it may be described as extending from about 9° to 16° N lat., and from 35° to 41° E long.; and it is bounded on the N by the territories of the pastoral Bidjas; on the E by those of the Danákil or Adaiel; on the S by Guráge and the territories of the Gallas, which formerly constituted a portion of the empire; and on the W by Sennár. Within these limits we may perhaps assign to A. a superficial area of 150,000 sq. m. But some geographers include the coasts of the Red sea, from Massówa, and even a degree further N, to Tajurrah beyond the straits of Bab-el- Mandeb on the S, within the limits of A.; while they extend its southern frontiers to the 8th parallel. Names.] This country corresponds to the southern part of the Æthiopia supra Ægyptum of the ancients; Nubia forming the northern. The Abessinians still call themselves Itiopaiwan, and their country Itiopia; but the names commonly applied to them are Abesha and Abeshi, from the Arabic Habesh, though they usually distinguish themselves according to their several provinces: as people of Tigré, Amhara, &c. Divisions.] Our topographical information regarding A. is not very certain; but the following may be regarded as the present divisions of the country:- I. Tigre', or Northern Abessinia, comprehending the tract betwixt the Red sea and the Takkazie; bounded N by the Mareb river, which separates it from the country of the Bekla, Boja, Takue, and Shankala tribes; E by the Shoho, Dankali, Hasaorta, and Galla tribes; S by the country of the Waag Agaus; and W by the Takkazie. Its subdivisions are: 1. Tigré Proper, forming the middle of the plateau. Chief towns, Adowa and Axum. 2. Agamé, to the E of Tigré Proper. Chief town, Adegrat. 3. Haramat, to the S of Agamé. 4. Enderta, to the S of Haramat. Chief town, Antálo. 5. Wodjerat, to the SE of Enderta, a wild district covered with forests. 6. Abergale, a low district along the E bank of the Takkazie, principally peopled by Agaus. 7. Salowa, a small province to the S of Enderta, and reaching to the Tselári, which divides it from Bora. 8. Shiré, to the W of Axum, and E of the Takkazie, the bed of which river has here an elevation of 2,600 ft. 10. Seráwi, a plain district N of Tigré Proper, from which it is separated by the Mareb. 11. Hamasen, forming the NE mountain-terrace of A., and overlooking the flat coast of the Red sea, near Harkíko (Arkeeko). This was formerly known as the Midre Bahr-nagash, i. e. 'the country of the Sea-king,' and was practically independent of any Abessinian chief; but it is now, like the whole of Tigré, subject to the rule of Dedjatch Ubie, the powerful prince of Samen. II. AMHARA, a high table-land sloping towards Sennár on the W, and comprehending the provinces t the W of the Takkazie, viz.: 1. Amhara Proper, to the SE of Begemder, now in the hands of the Edjow or Yedju Gallas. 2. Dembea, surrounding Lake Tsana, and containing Gondar, the capital of the empire. 3. Godjam, a powerful and independent state, S of Lake Tsana and the Abai, and forming the eastern portion of the peninsula surrounded by that river. Its capital is Bichana. 4. Damot, to the W of Godjam, also included within the bend of the Abai. Capital Burie, or now perhaps Gúdera. 5. Walaka, to the E of Godjam, now in the hands of the Tuloma Gallas. 6. Begemder, or Biégamider, to the N of Amhara Proper, and on the E side of Lake Tsana. Its capital, Debra Tabor, founded by Ras Guksa, is the residence of his grandson, Ras Ali. 7. Angot, an important and extensive province to the SE of Lasta, but little known. It is in the occupation of Mahommedan Yedju Gallas. To the S of it is Lake Haik. It forms a portion of the eastern edge of the table-land, and its elevation cannot be less than 8,000 or 9,000 ft. Dr. Krapf passed through it in 1842. 8. Samen, or Simen, to the W of the Takkazie, the most elevated district in Abessinia, consisting of an irregular mass of volcanic rocks, the highest points of which nearly reach the line of perpetual congelation. Its principal town or village is Entchetkab, which has an elevation of 10,000 ft. above sea-level. 9. Waldubba, to the NE of Samen, extolled as one of the granaries of A. 10. Walkayt, between Waldubba and Sennár. III. WAAG AND LASTA.] In consequence of this portion of A. having been temporarily subjected to Ras Walda Selássie of Tigre in the time of Salt, it was included by that traveller within that division of the empire; but geographically and ethnologically, not less than politically, it is entitled to be classed separately. Our knowledge of this country is principally derived from Dr. Beke, who traversed it in the beginning of 1843. Dr. Krapf, who passed along its eastern frontiers in 1842, says that "the Agaus differ as much from the rest of A. in their features, manners, and customs, as in their language," agreeing with them only in their profession of the Christian religion. Dr. Beke looks on the Agaus as the aboriginal inhabitants of A., who have retreated into the mountains and the valleys of the rivers before the advances of the present inhabitants of Tigre and Amhara, whose original seat was in Arabia. The Agau traditional history of A. differs in many important respects from that recorded in the written Ethiopic works, and on the whole appears to be deserving of the greater credit. The Waag-Shums, or rulers of Waag, are not subjects of the emperors of A, and even in the most flourishing times of the Being, whom they call Wak, i. e. 'Heaven.' They empire were always treated as independent sovereigns. But at the present day, the powerful arm of Ras Ali, added to an alliance with him by marriage, has made the Waag-Shum virtually his dependent. The provinces of Waag are: 1. Waag Proper, of which the capital, Sókota, is the residence of the Waag-Shum. 2. Dáhana, to the SW of Waag, and extending into the valley of the Tákkazie. 3. Búgana, to the S of Dáhana, and towards the head of the Tákkazie. 4. Wóffila, a high district E of Waag, in which is the lake of Ashangi. 5. Bora, to the N of Waag, and separated from Salowa in Tigre by the river Tselári. 6. Lasta Proper, which extends SE towards Angot, and is governed by princes who are subjects of the empire, appears to have been formerly a part of the dominions of the Waag- Shums. This portion of A., from its extremely mountainous character, is almost inaccessible to an invading army, which is doubtless the reason why it has been able to maintain its independence. It is highly deserving of more thorough investigation. IV. SHOA AND EFAT, an independent kingdom, which has been governed by its own hereditary sovereigns during a period of a century and a half. Sáhela Selássie, the sixth in descent from Negássie, the founder of the dynasty, is said to be just dead. In 1841, a mission was sent to him from the British government, under Major Harris. Efat lies between the 9th and 11th parallels of N lat. Its capital is Ankóbar, which, according to Dr. Beke, has an elevation of 8,200 ft. above sea-level. Shoa lies to the W of Efat. Its capital is Angolalla, the elevation of which above the sea is 8,400 ft., according to Dr. Beke. Tegulet, the ancient residence of the emperors of A., but now deserted, is in Shoa. M. Rochet estimates the population at 1,500,000. We should not greatly err perhaps in assigning to the kingdom of Shoa, which comprises Efat, an area of 20,000 sq. m., inclusive of its dependencies in the S. V. THE COUNTRY OF THE GALLAS may be here described, though not included within the limits of A. as above defined. The Gallas are very numerous in Abessinia, and inhabit also a large extent of country immediately to the S of it, and along the tablelands of Central Africa, of which the general character is similar to that of Shoa and Damot. The origin of the Gallas is involved in great obscurity. "The tradition is universal among them as to their having first come from Bargáma, which is understood to mean beyond the bahr or sea, i. e. 'a great lake in the interior of Africa." [Beke.] Attracted by the increasing fertility of the country, they gradually advanced northwards, remaining in the neighbourhood of Melinda for about two centuries. Dr. Beke says that many of the slaves imported into A. from the Galla countries have a lighter complexion than the Abessinians themselves, and some are scarcely darker than the natives of Southern Europe. The women exhibited in the slave-bazaars of Egypt under the name of Abessinians, and remarkable for their delicate features and finely proportioned figures, are most of them Gallas. They anoint their bodies with butter. Their war expeditions are generally performed on horseback; their principal weapon being a lance armed with an iron point. They are divided into numerous tribes, each of which has its own chief; but in Enárea, Guma, and some other districts, the absolute power is vested in a king. Polygamy is known amongst them. Their religion is what is called a natural one. They worship an invisible Almighty regard him as the creator and giver of all things, and therefore address their prayers chiefly to him. [Isenberg.] But many idolatrous practices prevail among them, and they also worship subordinate deities. Dr. Beke found among the tribes immediately S of the Abai the Christian religion so completely paganized as scarcely to have retained any traces of its origin. A lexicon and grammar of the Galla language has been recently published by Dr. Tutschek. Mountains.] A. is entirely a country of mountains or cluster of table-lands; and presents peculiar and picturesque features to the eye of the traveller. As a whole, it may be regarded as an immense tableland, of great elevation, but having a gentle inclination towards the NW, where it sinks down into the level countries of Sennár and Kordofan. On the S the limits of this table-land are undefined, but it is not unlikely that it extends beyond the equator. A lofty range, called Taranta, runs parallel to the coast of the Red sea, forming, at a distance of from 30 to 60 m., a buttress to the table-land on that side. This littoral range consists of slate and gneiss rock. The same formation of slate prevails throughout the whole country westwards. The same range, forming the edge of the central table-land, continues under the 40th parallel of longitude, from N to S, through Angot and Efat. From the eastern slopes of this ridge numerous streams descend to the Hawash. The mountains of Samen, which form an isolated mass, are still more elevated. To these we may add the mountains of Godjam, which give rise to the Abai; and the high land of Lasta, in which are the sources of the Takkazie. Salt, in the month of April, observed snow on two of the mountains of Samen; and Pearce, while crossing the same mountains, was overtaken by a snow-storm in the month of October. It seems clear, therefore, that if they do not attain they at least approach the limit of perpetual congelation; and Rüppell represents the Buahat as clad in eternal snow, having an elevation of 14,364 English ft.; while the Abba-Yared, a few miles to the eastward of the Buahat, is about 15,000 ft. The configuration of the Abessinian mountains is peculiar: they shoot up in sharp peaks, and in strange and fantastical forms. The shores of the Red sea are bordered with coral islands rising about 12 ft. above sea-level; and the coast is in many parts a continuation of these coral banks; but as the base of the mountain approaches very nearly to the edge of the sea, this flat slip of land is, in some places, not half-aleague in breadth. [Rüppell.] Rivers.] Abessinia is well-watered. Its most celebrated river is the Bahr-el-Azrek, or Abai (Astapus), which has its source within the peninsula of Godjam, at an elevation of 8,975 ft. [Beke.] It flows N and NE into Lake Tsana, across the southern extremity of which it passes without mixing its waters, so that its current remains always visible. It afterwards sweeps in a semicircular course, SE, S, and W, round the provinces of Godjam, Damot, and Agaumider, having its extreme southern limit about the 10th parallel. It then flows in a WNW direction through Sennár, till at Khartúm, in lat. 15° 34', and at an elevation of 1,525 ft. [Russegger], it unites with the Bahr-el-Abiad, or principal branch of the Nile .- Next in importance is the Takkazie (Astaboras), which, rising in Lasta, drains the western mountains of Samen, separates Tigré from Amhara, and flows into the Nile in the district of Berber, forming the NE boundary of the ancient kingdom of Meroe. Besides these, there are the Arékwa, the Angrab, the Bellegas, the Mareb, the Anguya, and the Tselári, which fall into the Tákkazie; the Báshilo, Djamma, and Muger, which join the Abai; the Dedhésa, which also unites with the latter river, and with it forms the Bahr-el-Azrek, of which indeed the Dedhésa is the direct stream; the Yabús, Tumat, Dender, and Rahad, which fall into the Bahr-el-Azrek; and the Hawash, which is the principal river of Shoa, rising in the country of the Mécha (Maitsha) Gallas, flowing from SW to NE, running through the southern part of Shoa, and emptying itself into Lake Abhebbad by Aussa. The falls of the rivers of A. are remarkable. In a distance of 25 m. between the cataract of Tisesat ('the smoke of fire'), which falls perpendicularly 100 ft., and the Broken bridge in the NE of Godjam, the Abai has a difference of level of 2,000 ft. [Beke]; while the Chacha, one of the head-streams of the Djamma, has a fall of not less than 4,245 ft. in about the same distance. [Rochet.] There are several lakes, of which that of Tsana or Dembea is the chief. Its length is about 60 m. Ludolf says there are eleven islands in it. Other lakes are Zuwai, in Guráge, Haik, in N lat. 11° 25', and Ashangi, in the district of Wofila. Climate.] Along the shores of the Red sea the heat is great, but on the table-land the temperature is moderate, and seldom disagreeably warm, and some of the provinces enjoy a more temperate climate than Portugal or Spain. The thermometrical register kept at Ankober by Mr. Kirk, attached to Major Harris's mission, in 1841 and 1842, shows that in the shade "the greatest difference between the mean temperature of the coldest month (December) and the hottest (June) was only 10°; whilst the greatest range during the whole year between the minimum, 41°, and maximum, 69°, amounted only to 28°; the mean annual temperature having been 55\(\frac{1}{2}\) in latitude 9° 35' N." Thunder is frequent and violent; and whirlwinds frequently occur. The rainy season on the table-land lasts from June to September, during which time the country is drowned by a continual deluge. On the low lands along the sea-coast the rains prevail more or less from October to April. The finest months of the year are those of December and January. The Kolla, as the low country in the valleys of the rivers is called, is much dreaded on account of its unhealthy character. Soil.] On the mountains the soil is sometimes fertile, but more frequently barren. In Tigré the soil is sandy. More to the S it is principally of a heavy argillaceous description, formed by the disintegration of the trachytic and other volcanic rocks. In the valleys, particularly of the Abai and its branches, the soil is highly fertile. Agriculture is in a rude state. The plough consists of a stout pole shod with iron, and is drawn by oxen. They mow and reap with a short toothed sickle; and the grain is trampled out of the ear by oxen. The low lands generally produce two or three crops in the year. Productions. Gifted with abundant water, a rich and varied soil, and a delicious climate, vegetation never sleeps in this favoured region. Rice is unknown; wheat, barley, oats, rye, and pulse are raised in considerable quantities; but in the lower grounds the heat is too great for these grains, and there the crops are chiefly of Mashila (Sorghum vulgare). Teff (Poa Abyssinica), on the contrary, grows on every soil, and affords the bread which is in universal use. The reason given to Dr. Beke by a debtera or scribe for this preference of a grass seed to other grain, was that when the country was devastated in the beginning of the 16th century by the Mahommedan invader Ahmed Gran, the people were prevented from tilling their fields, and were forced to subsist on the seeds of natural grasses, and that, becoming accustomed to the taste, they afterwards cultivated them. On some of the lowest grounds, unfit even for the production of teff, is raised another grass called dágusa (Elusine Tocussa), which yields a black bread. "The emperor of Gondar is said to be forced by etiquette to eat cakes of it, to the exclusion of other farinaceous food." [Roth.] Capsicums are an important object of cultivation throughout A., as forming a staple article of food. Coffee grows wild in many of the warmer provinces, as does also a species of tea (Celastrus spec.), the fresh leaves of which are chewed, especially by the Mahommedans. The cotton of A. is short-stapled, but soft and delicate. Many species of Indigofera grow wild and unheeded; flax is cultivated merely for the seeds, of which oil is made, but the Nug (Polymnia Abyssin.) is the chief oil plant. The Kolqual is a remarkable Euphorbia, in its growth resembling an immense chandelier. Other vegetable productions are, the papyrus, the grape, lemon, tamarind, and citron; the plant ensete, a species of Urania, said by Bruce to afford the very best of vegetable food; but Dr. Roth states, that "though in Guráge the young shoots of the ensete form a principal part of the diet, they are despised by the Abessinians;" the kosso (Hagenia Abyssinica), one of the most beautiful and useful of trees, its flowers being a drastic used to expel the tænia, to which all persons are subject; the daro tree, and the white and yellow jasmine. Dr. Beke found dog-roses, honeysuckles, nettles, and butter-cups in Efat. Firs, junipers, and wild olives clothe the upper slopes of the mountains, while bananas and the sugar-cane thrive in the sultry recesses of the valleys. The sycamores often exceed 20 ft., and the majestic baobab sometimes reaches 80 ft., in circumference. A singular plant named jibarra (Rhynchopetalum montanum), mimics the figure of the palm in the neighbourhood of perpetual snow in the prov. of Samen, and also on the summits of the mountains of Efat and Godjam. From the great variety of its surface, and the uncultivated state of some of its districts, A. possesses a number of wild animals. Among these is the spotted hyena (Hyæna crocuta), called here djib, which makes nightly inroads into villages and towns; "they fight the dogs, and for want of other prey drag off some of them. On the borders of the low country, the night camps must be fenced round with thorns as a protection against their inroads." [Roth.] Elephants and rhinoceroses are numerous in the low grounds of A., in the valleys of the principal rivers, and also in the Galla countries S of the Abai. The buffalo, domesticated in Egypt and elsewhere, is here one of the most ferocious of animals. Hippopotami (gomári) and crocodiles abound in the rivers and lakes; the thick skin of the former is cut into shields and whips. The lion is met with occasionally, and there are several species of leopards, one of which is black. The zebra is frequent in the southern provinces; and numerous monkeys inhabit the crags and precipices .- The domestic animals are not very different from those of Europe. The most remarkable is the Galla ox (sanga), bearing horns of enormous magnitude and extremely disproportioned to its body. Mr. Salt saw one horn 4 ft. long, and 21 inches in circumference at its root. The great antelope with spirally curved horns is occasionally seen. The horses are strong and beantiful. Small black sheep, and white finehaired cattle, are abundant; and there are numerous herds of wild swine. Dogs are very numerous, but not a tenth part possess owners. The feathered creation in A. bears more than its usual proportion to the other kinds of animal life, and presents the most varied and gorgeous plumage. Within a circuit of 4 or 5 m. round Harkíko (Arkeeko), 132 different kinds of birds were procured by Dr. Rüppell's hunters in the course of a month. The nisser, or golden eagle-perhaps the largest bird of the old continent—and a beautiful species called the black eagle, are particularly noticed by Bruce. To these Salt adds a new species, called goodie-goodie, l the size of the common falcon; and the blue heron, the flamingo, and the white ibis. Storks, snipes, redlegged partridges, pigeons, swallows, honey-suckers, bee-eaters, parrots, finches, thrushes, fly-catchers, and owls, occur in great number and variety.-Among insects, the most numerous and useful are bees. Honey constitutes everywhere an important article, it being used to make the common drink of the upper classes. The locust commits ravages in A. nearly as terrible as in other countries of Northern Africa. Serpents are not numerous. A fly is found whose sting is said to be dreaded even by the lion, and a destructive species of black ant. Fish are abundant in the rivers, and likewise on the coasts of the Red sea. The mineralogy of A. is very imperfectly known. With the exception of iron, which is plentiful and of excellent quality, it seems remarkably destitute of metals; for the gold which passes through the country is brought from the barbarous regions to the S and W. One of the most important natural productions of the country is salt. A great plain of salt, called Arho, occupies part of the tract to the E of Agame, between Hánfila and Massówa. It covers a valley running from SE to NW, and is a day's journey wide, and four days' journey in length. It is perfectly pure and hard to the depth of nearly 2 ft.; but that lying beneath is coarser and softer till purified by exposure to the air. Its fine crystallization and regular fracture admit of its being cut into pieces like a mower's whet-stone, which not only serve for seasoning to food, but also circulate as money. About 30 m. to the W of Tajurra is a salt lake, known as the Bahr-Assal, the salt from which is carried by the Danákil to Shoa, where it is used for domestic purposes. The surface of this lake was found by Dr. Beke in 1840, and after him by other travellers, to be from 600 to 700 ft. below the sea-level. Population.] The Weimar Almanach for 1844 estimates the population of A. at 4,500,000; Ungewitter at 5,000,000. The Abessinians generally are in their persons tall and well-made, with an oval contour of countenance, a thin pointed nose, well-set teeth, and smooth or slightly curled hair. They are of a dark olive, or bronze complexion, but have nothing of the Negro features. They have a great resemblance to the Arabs, with whom some at least have a cognate origin. Rüppell says their physiognomy is identical with that of the Bedouin Arabs, and that the greater part of the inhabitants of Samen and of the plains around Lake Dembea, as well as the Falashas and Agaus, belong physically to the same family. But this can hardly be taken in an unqualified sense, inasmuch as they bear unquestionable marks of their African origin, most of them having thick lips, and some almost woolly hair. Their dress consists of a large piece of cotton-cloth, which they wrap round them like a mantle, and to which they add close drawers reaching to the middle of the thigh, with a girdle of cloth. In the S the drawers are worn quite loose, while among the Gallas this garment takes the form of a short petticoat. The Christians of A. wear a string of twisted silk of a dark blue colour round the neck, for which, however, a string of twisted cotton is often substituted. Their habitations are, for the most part, huts of a conical form, with thatched roofs. The houses of the sovereign and a few of the grandees are large and commodious; but the principal display of architectural magnificence is upon the churches, which are very numerous. They are always of a circular form, and their thatched roofs, projecting considerably beyond the walls, are supported by wooden pillars forming a species of colonnade. The manners of the Abessinians are rude, and they seem not to know the value of cleanliness. Indeed, it is almost a matter of religion among the Christians not to be too rigid in their ablutions, lest they should be taken for Mahommedans. [Beke.] They anoint their hair with butter, which becoming rancid has a most offensive smell. The state of morals is very low. Marriages are rarely contracted in the face of the church, and the alliances formed are in fact little more than temporary unions. The consequences are most fatal as regards their social and domestic relations. Gobat says, that "the children of one and the same father, who are not of the same mother, are always enemies to each other." Nor do either party consider themselves bound to observe, with rigid fidelity, this slight engagement even while it lasts. When to this we add, that the Abessinians live in an almost uninterrupted state of civil war, it must cease to excite wonder if we find them losing every portion of the partial civilization which they once possessed, and verging to complete barbarism. Yet cruelty does not form a marked feature in the A. character; and in war they seldom kill their prisoners. [Gobat.] Indeed, their ordinary exclamation on approaching a foe, "You are pardoned," is a convincing proof that no one needs to be killed, or even to fight, if only content to submit and be plundered. [Beke.] And Rüppell testifies that throughout A. slaves are treated with great mildness. Redeeming traits in the character of the Abessinians are their hospitality, charity, and forgiveness of injuries - if indeed the latter, carried as it is to an excess, may not be regarded as a fault, and as a main cause of the evils of their social condition. A vassal may rebel against his ruler, a servant offend his master, and he has only to take sanctuary in one of the numerous "cities of refuge," or in the house of a friend, to obtain the mediation of an influential intercessor, and he is pardoned, only that he may repeat his offence at the first convenient opportunity. [Beke.] Salt thinks that the people of Tigre are ruder in their habits, and fiercer than those of Amhara; but Gobat says that in Tigre "the women are much more reserved than in the interior." The chief food of the natives consists of thin cakes of teff bread, with the addition of various dishes in which red pepper forms the principal ingredient. The flesh of oxen and sheep is often added, which is sometimes cooked, but more frequently eaten raw, and at the tables of the princes and chiefs while the warmth of life is still in it. However, Bruce's description of the cutting up of the living animal can only be regarded as a mere fiction. [Beke.] Pearce, who was several years in Abessinia, declares that he never heard of such feasts as Bruce describes. Beke, who was long resident in the furthest provinces of A., expresses his conviction that no animal is eaten unless slaughtered "in the name of the Father, Son, and Holy Ghost," and the blood allowed to run on the ground, though afterwards the sooner the meat can be cut from the carcase the greater delicacy it is considered. And Gobat says: "I admit that such a feast may have taken place among the most shameless libertines; but excesses of that kind are not customary." The most general drink is a species of sour beer, called bouza in Tigre and talla in Amhara, made from fermented cakes. Hydromel (Tigre mes or maize, Amh. tzadj) is also made in great quantities. Tribes. The inhabitants of A. comprise a variety of tribes, all however resembling each other more or less in physical character and in manners. Besides the Abessinians, as the inhabitants of Tigre and Amhara are usually called, though they cannot be regarded as the aborigines of the country, Modern A. contains the Agaus of Waag and of Agaumider, who tants; the Falashas, of cognate origin, who are scattered among the Christians, principally in Samen, and in the neighbourhood of Gondar and Tchelga to the NW of Lake Tsana, and still profess the religion of Moses: they are the smiths, masons, potters, and thatchers of the country; the Zalans, Waitos, and Kamants, all located in the provinces surrounding the lake, are tribes possessing peculiar habits, being apparently remains of the aboriginal people; the Gallas; the Shankalas, a woolly-headed, deep black race, the Troglodytes of the ancients; the Shohos, Hazaorte, and Dankali, along the coast of the Red sea; the Somális; and several other savage tribes. Religion.] The Abessinians are professedly Christians of the Coptic church, subject to some modifications; but from the gross ignorance of the priests, scarcely less than that of the laity, Christianity is here, by a strange mixture of Judaical and Pagan observances, rendered utterly inefficient for renewing the heart or improving the life. They are, moreover, divided into three parties holding different views of the nature of Christ. They use a liturgy, and have a translation of the Scriptures—the ancient Ethiopic version—alleged to have been made by Frumentius. The patriarch of Alexandria is the head of the Abessinian church; and from him the Abuna or resident bishop, who is always a Coptic priest, receives his investiture. They have monasteries both of monks and nuns; but their professions of austerity are not very rigidly kept. Their veneration for the Virgin is unbounded; and their saints are numerous, and, for miraculous powers, leave those of the Romish calendar far behind. They have no fewer than 180 festivals in the year, and their fasts extend over nearly nine months of the twelve. The marriage of priests, who are not monks, is allowed as in the Greek church; but a second marriage renders them unfit for the sacred office. Persons who are unclean according to the Levitical law, may not enter the churches. Like the Greek communion, the Abessinians banish from their places of worship all works of sculpture; but paintings are numerous, being mostly rude copies from European or Egyptian originals, some of which are still occasionally to be met with. They receive the sacrament of the Lord's supper in both kinds. They baptize male children after 40 days, and female after 80. A ceremony which may be called purification by water, and which is performed yearly, induced the Jesuits to assert that in A. every person was annually baptized. Tellez says that the Falashas still possess the Hebrew scriptures; but this may be doubted.— Mahommedans are on the increase in A., most of the relations of Ras Ali being of this religion. Language and Literature. The Geez or Ethiopic, in which the books of the Abessinians are written, and of which a dialect is still spoken in Tigre, is cognate with the Arabic. "It approaches nearest to the Arabic, of which it seems to be a kind of production, as being comprehended almost within the same grammatical rules, the same forms of conjugations, the same forms of plurals both entire and anomalous; so that whoever understands either that or the rest of the oriental languages, may, with little labour, understand this our Ethiopic." [Ludolf.]—The Amharic language, which has been used at the court since the 14th century, is softer than the Geez, and is spoken through at least one-half of A., has also many Arabic roots. Its alphabet is the same as the Geez, with six additional letters. Gobat thinks that about a fifth of the male population speaking Amharic can read. The Agaus speak a distinct language, which, according to Beke, exists in three separate dialects, as spoken in Waag, in Agaumider, and among the Falashas, and also probably among the other aboriginal tribes. The are the true representatives of the original inhabi- | Abessinians possess the Constitutions of the Apostles from the Arabic, -some of the Greek fathers, -the Synaxar, containing the lives of their saints, and the Prophecies of Enoch. A translation of the last-mentioned work was published by Dr. Lawrence in 1822. Dr. Krapf has formed a list of nearly 200 works written principally in Ethiopic, a few only being in Amharic. Copies of a considerable number of these collected by him are deposited in the libraries of the British museum and of the university of Tübingen. In computing time, the Abessinians make use of the solar year, consisting of 12 months of 30 days, and 5 (or 6) intercalary days called pagmie, from the Greek epagomena. They name their years after the Evangelists, that of St. John being the bisextile. The year commences at present on Sept. 10th of our calendar. Government. The code of laws under which the empire is governed is the Fetha-Negest, the original of which is said to have fallen from heaven in the time of Constantine. But the severity of this code is usually modified, the governors of provinces being at liberty to decide arbitrarily. "Formerly all important cases were brought before the king, and his twelve leecs, -a kind of ministry chosen and dismissed by the king, who, in his turn, could do nothing without them. They alone had the right to judge in criminal matters; but at present they preserve nothing but the title, and the right to judge, in some particular cases, at Gondar only." [Gobat.] A murderer is put to death, or ransomed, at the will of the relations of the murdered party. The sovereignty is both hereditary and elective, -hereditary in a family, but elective among its various branches; and thus the Abyssinians entail upon themselves all the evils of both systems without the advantages of either. "At the time of Bruce, the kings were possessed of a little power; and at the time of Salt they had some appearance of it; but since the death of Ras Guksa they have had nothing but the title of king. They would have lost even this, were it not that the governors stand in need of it, to support that of the Ras; for a dedjazmatch cannot take the title of Ras, deserts. lawfully, unless he has placed a new king on the throne." [Gobat.] From the abdication of Tekla Haimanot in 1778 down to 1833, no less than 14 princes have occupied the throne of A. at twenty-two different times. Civil war thus rages almost without intermission; and there is little doubt that the pasha of Egypt—who is understood to have meditated the conquest of A. while effecting that of Nubia-would have easily accomplished his object but for the interference of England. Manufactures and Commerce.] A. has little of manufacturing industry, or commercial activity. A few articles of indispensable necessity are fabricated within the country. Cotton-cloths, the universal dress of the country, are made in all the provinces of the empire, chiefly by Mahommedan artisans: at Gondar a peculiarly fine sort is made with beautiful silk borders. The borders are usually plain blue or red cetton; but being unable to dye those colours, they unravel blue Surat or Turkey red cloths, and weave them again into their own webs. Manufactures of iron and brass are also considerable, the iron being of native production and the brass imported. They also work in silver, but to no great extent. Spears, swords, and knives are made in the country; and in Shoa they even manufacture fire-arms with the exception of the barrel. Hides are tanned to great perfection in Tigré. Sheep-skins are tanned in some places, and also made into parchment. Shoa exports hides and grain to the neighbouring countries. Dr. Beke says that throughout Shoa and the Galla country the state of manufactures is much superior to that of A. properly so called. Cloth, iron, and cattle, are imported into A. from the Galla Migitized by Google countries; also ivory and coffee from Enárea, and gold and civet. The foreign commerce of Abessinia is carried on by way of Massówa, in the N, and Tadjurra in the S. Camels are used to convey mer-<br>chandise over the low desert country between the sea and the edge of the table land; but they can go no further, and the goods have afterwards to be carried on mules and asses, or on men's heads. The total value of the exports and imports of Massowa about 25 years ago was £80,000 per ann. The exports were more recently stated at £41,700, of which slaves formed the principal item, amounting to 6,000 at 60 dollars per head, or £24,000. The next item was gold-dust, valued at £8,000. Among other articles of export are coffee, civet, myrrh, ivory, furs-consisting chiefly of lion, tiger, panther, and black leopard skins-and ostrich feathers. The coffee is chiefly from Enarea and the districts to the S and W of Shoa. The foreign trade of Tadjurra with Shoa is of still less amount, the exports consisting chiefly of slaves; but there is a considerable trade between the latter country and the Dankáli tribes, who carry up salt and receive grain in exchange. The commercial productions of the A. of the present day appear to be nearly the same which this country offered to Egypt in early times. The imports were lead, blocktin, gold-foil, Persian carpets, raw silks from China, French broad cloths, coloured skins from Egypt, glassbeads and drinking bottles from Venice. But the present poverty of the country will not allow the introduction of many articles of luxury. The staple manufactures, cloth and hardware, are such as British industry could probably execute better and cheaper for the Abessinians than they can do themselves; and it is likely that a much more extensive market in the interior might be opened through Abessinia; for Cobbeh, the capital of Darfur, which is 900 m. from Siút, is only between 500 and 600 m. from Gondar, and the greater part of the road is through a fertile country, instead of inhospitable History.] The Ethiopian work entitled 'Kebir za Negest,' being a chronicle of the kings of Abessinia, goes back to a period considerably earlier than the Christian era. From Arwe, the Serpent-king, to Menilek, the alleged son of the queen of Sheba by Solomon king of Israel, this chronicle counts five kings, or rather dynasties. The 8th king, subsequent to the Christian era, was Za-Hakala, or Soskalus. About the year 330 Christianity was introduced into A. by Frumentius, who was afterwards raised by Athanasius, patriarch of Alexandria, to the dignity of bishop. It would seem that the power of the Abessinian monarchs was well established in the 4th century, and that at that period they had extended their dominions into Arabia. About the year 530, several Christian missionaries proceeded to A. with the view of rekindling the expiring embers of this religion. About the same period, Caleb or Elesbaan, king of A., in alliance with the emperor Justinian, made several campaigns in Arabia against the Koreishites, and conquered a part of Yemen. Sixty years after this, the Abessinians were expelled from Arabia by the Persians, who drove them across the Red sea, and conquered a large tract of country on the African side of that sea; but their mountains, and the physical features of their country, enabled the Abessinians to preserve their national existence and religion when assaulted by the Moslems. In 1368 the grandees of Shoa reinstated an alleged branch of the ancient dynasty of Sheba on the throne. Among the monarchs of this dynasty, Amda Zion was a warlike and powerful prince, in the beginning of the 14th century. It is probable that the Crusades were known to the Abessinians, for about their time we find their princes abstaining from the customary pilgrimage to Palestine, and despatching a bishop as their representative to visit the Holy tomb. This bishop, having fallen into the hands of the Mahommedans, was forced to adopt their faith, which gave rise to a long and bloody war betwixt the Moslems and Abessinians. In 1445 the Abessinian emperor. Zara Yacob, sent an ambassador to the council of Florence. The favourable reports which some Abessinian priests who visited Europe made of their native country and of its trade with the south of Africa, stimulated the Portuguese to those voyages in the course of which the Cape of Good Hope and India were discovered. Pedro Covilham, one of the Portuguese agents sent to discover the true Prester John, presented himself in 1499 at the court of the Abessinian negus, or king, then in Shoa, and prevailed on the iteghe, or queenmother, to send an ambassador to Portugal. This event produce ! a great sensation throughout Europe; the Portuguese court acknowledged the Abessinian envoy, and despatched a grand embassy on their part to the court of their new ally. A few years afterwards we find the Abessinian monarch assisted by a force of 400 Portuguese in repelling the attacks of Ahmed Gran (commonly called Mohammed Gragne), a powerful Mahommedan chief who reigned in the kingdom of Harrar, situated E of the province of Efat. Many Catholic missionaries obtained access to Abessinia during the subsistence of its relations with Portugal; but towards the end of the 16th century A. became less accessible to Europeans, the Turks having seized the ports of Suakim, Massowa, and Zeyla, and the Gallas making fierce inroads on the country. The Jesuit, Paez or Payz, went to A. in 1603. He visited and described the source of the Bahr-el-Azrek or Abai; and acquired such influence at court that the emperor embraced the Roman Catholic religion. But he was soon forced to renounce it; and his son Fasil or Basilides expelled all the agents of the Propaganda from the country. In 1699, when the French physician Poncet, visited Abessinia, the court had been transferred to Gondar. In 1750, three Franciscans penetrated to that city, and acquired considerable influence at the court of Yasu or Joshua II, who reigned from 1729 to 1752. In 1770 Bruce visited Abessinia. Its next European visitor was Salt, in 1805, and again in 1809. It has since been visited by Gobat, Combes and Tamisier, Rochet d'Hericourt, the brothers d'Abbadie, Rüppell, Von Katte, Harris, Krapf, Isenberg, and Beke. It is to the relations of these travellers—especially the last mentioned—that we owe our present acquaintance with this country. Authorities.] Ludolph's History of Ethiopia.—Bruce's Travels, 7 vols. 8vo.—Salt's Journal in Lord Valentia's Travels, 1809. 3 vols. 4to.—Salt's Voyage to Abyssinia, 4to. 1814.—Gobat's Journal of a Three Years' Residence in Abyssinia. 1834. 12mo.—Reise in Abyssinien, von Dr. E. Rüppell. 1838. 2 vols. 8vo.- Journals of the Rev. Messrs. Isenberg and Krapf. 8vo. 1843.—Rochet, Voyage à Choa. 8vo. 1842.—D'Abbadie, in Bull. Geogr. de Paris.-Highlands of Æthiopia. By Major Harris. 1844. 3 vols. 8vo.—Abessinien und die evangelische Mission, von C. W. Isenberg. 2 vols. 12mo.—Beke's Papers in Geogr. Journ.; Edin. Phil. Journ.; Bull. Geog. de Paris, &c.
ABZAC, a town of France, in the dep. of Charente, 6 m. N of Confolens. Pop. 1,126.
ABZAL. See ABI-ZAL.

ACADIA, the name originally given by the French to Nova Scotia and New Brunswick, and, in the charter of Henry IV. of France, to the whole N American continent from the 40th to the 46th parallel of lat. The name is said to have been derived from that of the Shubenacadie, one of the principal rivers of Nova Scotia.

ACADIAN MOUNTAINS, a range on the N American continent, running to the E of N between N lat. 41° and 49°, and forming the eastern boundary of the Great N American plain. They have their northern termination in the peninsula of Gaspe at the mouth of the St. Lawrence. Their total length is nearly 600 m., with a breadth varying from 70 to 150 m. On their eastern side, between them and the Atlantic, extends an undulating and hilly district varying from 50 to 80 m. in breadth. The most elevated points in this mountain-system occur in the White Mountains, in New Hampshire, which, in Mount Washington, attain an elevation of 6,428 ft., in Mount Adams 5,328 ft., and in Mount Jefferson 5,058 ft. In the Green mountains, in Vermont, another branch of the A. system, Mount Mansfield has an altitude of 4,279 ft., and Camel's Rump of 4,188 ft. above sealevel. But perhaps still loftier summits connected with this great mountain-system may yet be discovered in the ranges running off into high parallels on the NE.

ACADIE, a district of Lower Canada, stretching along the W side of the river St. John and the N frontier of the state of New York. It is about 221 m. in length, by 20 in breadth; and has a superficial area of 240 sq. m. The pop., chiefly of French descent, is nearly 12,000. Besides the Montreal river, which intersects it, it is watered by La Tortue and La Colle.
ACAJUTLA, a harbour of Guatimala, to the SW ACA of San Salvador. It is the port of Sonsonate, but is merely an open bay without shelter. Lat. 13° 37' N.
ACAM. See AKIM.

ACAMAS (CAPE), or St. EPIPHANI, or PIFANIO, a promontory forming the NW extremity of the island of Cyprus, on which there is a village of the same name. N lat. 35° 6', E long. 32° 16'. It bears from Cape Tranquillo, the S extremity of the island, ESE [E by S] nearly 74 leagues. It is sometimes known as Cape Salizano.
ACAMBARO, an Indian village of Mexico, in the prov. of Queretaro.
ACAMEA, an affluent of the Parima in Guayana.
ACANCAN, a river of Venezuela, flowing NW into the Caroni river.
ACAPALA, a village of Mexico, in the prov. of Tabasco, 45 m. N of Villa Hermosa.
ACAPONETA, a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Xalisco, in N lat. 22° 34', W long. 105° 18'.

ACAPU'LCO, or Los Reyes, a city and seaport of Mexico, on a small bay of the Pacific; in N lat. 16° 50', W long. 99° 46' [Humboldt]; W long. 99° 54' [Capt. B. Hall]; 99° 50′ 44″ or 5° 24′ E of San Francisco [Beechey]; 99° 52′ 15" [Beleher in 1837]; Fort San Diego 16° 50′ 48" N, 99° 52' W [Raper, 3d ed.]. Humboldt describes the bay as an immense basin, excavated in granite rocks, and measuring nearly 20,000 ft. in breadth from E to W; and pronounces it one of the finest ports in the world. Captain Basil Hall says of it: "It is the very beau ideal of a harbour. It is easy of access; very capacious; the water is not too deep; the holding-ground good; quite free from hidden dangers, and as secure as the basin in the centre of Portsmouth dockyard." From the interior of the harbour the sea cannot be discovered; and a stranger coming to the spot by land would imagine he was looking over a sequestered mountainlake. The bay has two entrances, on either side of the island Roqueta. That on the N side is known as the Boca Chica, or narrow entrance, its breadth not exceeding 1,000 ft. The other has a fair average breadth of 1½ m., with from 24 to 33 fathoms water. -In the following chart of the port of A., from Sir E. Belcher's Survey of 1837, A is the Boca Chicha; b Punta Grifo; C the city of A.; d the castle of San Diego; e the Piedras Blancas or White rocks; f Point Guitarron; g Point Bruja; h Port Marques; I the Great entrance; k the Roqueta. The city of Acapulco owes all its importance to its harbour, and to being the nearest port to Mexico city. Humboldt estimated the resident population at 4,000, chiefly people of colour, Chinese, Mulattoes, and Negroes. It stands on a bay on the W side of the roadstead, the N horn of which is formed by a small promontory surmounted by the formidable fortress of San Diego. A. is an unhealthy place from the combination of heat and moisture in the atmosphere, and its being surrounded by lofty mountains; and it does not seem well-situated for commerce. The passage from A. to Lima is long and | difficult; while that from Guyaquil to A. frequently occupies five weeks. Captain Hall sailed from Panama to A.-a distance of 1,495 m.-in 30 days, and it may be accomplished by steamers in 166 hours; but it is only from December to May inclusive, he says, that it is advisable to navigate this coast. The interior transit from A. to the city of Mexico, from which it is distant 183 m. SSW, is also difficult, being conducted across a rugged and barren country by a mule-track ascending 7,500 ft. in the total distance between the two cities. But for this laborious land-carriage—which is still more arduous between Vera Cruz and Mexico—the flour of Mexico could compete with that of North America in the British market. Yet A. once conducted a considerable foreign trade, particularly with Manilla, as all are aware who have read Anson's voyages. Its exports are silver, cochineal, indigo, and skins. The net proceeds of the customs at A. in 1841 was only 7,193 dollars, or about £1,620.
ACAQUIRY, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Balaghaut, 36 m. NW of Adoni.

ACARA', a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Pará, running in a N by W course into the estuary of the Para a few miles above the town of Para. It is in the lewer portion of its course "a quiet narrow river, winding among comparatively lofty banks, and through large and well-cultivated plantations." The Mojú to the W makes a common estuary with the A.

ACARABISI, a river of British Guayana, which, rising in N lat. 7° 20', flows in a SW course, through a swampy district, into the Cuyuni, in 6° 54', a few miles to the W. of Haiowa, at a point 260 ft. above sea-level.—Schomburgk.

ACARAGUA, or Acarigua, a town of Venezuela, in the prov. of Apure, near the Claro or Cojeda river on the road from Varinas to Puerto Cabello, 105 m. NE by N of Varinas, in N lat. 8° 40', W long. 68° 57'.
ACARAY, or ACARAGA, a river of Paraguay, flowing by a SE course into the Parana.

ACARI, a small river of Peru, in the prov. of Arequipa, rising on the W side of the Andes, and falling into the sea in S lat. 15° 25', W long. 75° 10'.—There is a town of the same name on its r. bank.
ACARIOS, a small island in the Grecian archipelago, 9 m. E of Naxos. Angelos.

ACARNA'NIA, an ancient division of Northern Greece; and a department of the modern kingdom of Greece, having boundaries nearly coincident with those of the ancient A. The modern prov. of A. is bounded on the N by the Ambracian gulf, commonly Kalocza. known as the gulf of Arta, and by Turkey. The boundary line with Turkey intersects the low sandy promontory of La Punta, so as to leave the fort of La Punta, which commands the entrance of the gulf to Turkey; and, from the E shore of La Punta traversing the gulf of Arta in a NE direction, giving the island of Kefalo and the group called Vouvalu to Greece, and skirting the coast-line at the NE corner of the gulf. It then runs ENE to the Doubsa river; and thence to the summit of the ridge called Chelona [alt. 6,312 ft. Baker], whence it is carried along an undulating ridge of bare rock to the summit of Mount Gabrovo [alt. 6,479 ft. Baker], from which it descends to the Aspropotamos, which forms the boundary of Acarnania throughout its whole length on the E and SE to its mouth; separating it from the prov. of Eurytania, Trichonia, and Ætolia. On the SW and W, A. is bounded along a coast-line of about 75 m. by that part of the Mediterranean known to ancient writers as the Ionian sea. Its superficial area is 5.59 new Greek sq. m. or 215 English sq. m. The pop. in 1840 was 25,083. A royal decree, in April 1833, united A. and Ætolia in one nomarchy, and the ACC 5 eparchies or subdivisions of Acarnania, Missolonghi, Naupactos, Agrinion, and Kallidrome; but in July 1838, this arrangement was superseded, and A. now forms one of the 24 departments of Greece, with Karbasera or Karbasari, on a bay of the same name, in the SE extremity of the gulf of Arta, as its capital. Its other towns are Dragomestre on a bay of the Ionian sea; Vonitza on the gulf of Arta; Azio, supposed by some to mark the site of the ancient Actium; Karpenitya, and Ambrakia. All these towns consist of little more than a few ill-built huts. The interior of A. is little known and its ancient geography presents many debatable points. The shores of the gulf of Arta are generally swampy. The hills in its vicinity, on the A. side, are roundtopped and barren. Olives and myrtles are prominent features in the natural vegetation of the country. Cultivation appears to be confined to the neighbourhood of the towns. Coal and sulphur exist within this territory.

ACASAGUATLAN, or ACASABASTLAN, a town of Guatemala, in the district and on a river of the same name, which here joins the Motagua; 45 m. E of New Guatemala; in N lat. 14° 58', W long. 89° 40'. The neighbourhood produces sarsaparilla, cocoa, and cassia. The district of Acasaguatlan, or Zacapa, is the western part of the old division of Chiquimula.
ACASATHULA, a seaport of Nicaragua, on a point of land forming a bay in the Pacific ocean, 12 m. W of Nicaragua.
ACASHE, a town of Nubia, on the r. bank of the Nile, in N lat. 21° 5′, E long. 30° 52′.
ACASSA, a river of French Guayana in the prov. of Cayenne, falling into the Atlantic at the estuary of the Oyapok, near Cape Orange.
ACASSA. See AKASSA.

ACASTER-MALBIS, a p. and township of England, in the E and W ridings of Yorkshire, partly in the wapentake of Ouse and Derwent, partly in the ainsty of York city. The township is 5 m. S by W of York, Pop. in 1841, 322.
ACASTER-SELBY, a township in the parish of Stillingfleet, in the Wriding of York, 7 m. NW from York. Pop. 188.
ACASUCHTITLAN, a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Puebla; 50 m. N from La Puebla de los
ACASUMPET, a town in the Carnatic, on the coast a few miles N of Pondicherry.
ACASZTO, a town of Lower Hungary, between the Danube and the Theiss; 15 m. NE by N from
ACATEPEC, an Indian settlement in New Mexico, occupying a spacious valley, and containing 860 Indian families. — There are several other small settlements bearing the same name in Mexico.
ACATLAN, a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Puebla, on the Tucad; 70 m. S of La Puebla de los Angelos.—Also the name of several settlements in New Mexico, chiefly occupied by Indian families.
ACAWAN, a town on the western borders of Fezzan; in N lat. 28° 14′, E long. 11° 27′.
ACAXUTLA, or ACAJUTLA, a small harbour or open bay in Guatimala, on the Pacific, 8 m. SW of Sonsonate.
ACAY, a town of La Plata, on the head-branch of the Guachipos river flowing along the E side of the cordillera De los Valles.

ACAYUCAN, a district of Mexico, in the prov. of Vera Cruz, near the coast of the Mexican gulf, containing about 300 Indian families. Its capital, of the same name, lies W of the river Coazacoalco, about 120 m. SE from Vera Cruz.
ACCABA. See AKABAH.
ACCABA, or AKABAH, a village of Nubia, on the caravan route between Berber and Es-suan, in N lat. 20° 50', E long. 34° 10'.
ACCAD, a village of Nubia, on the Mogren, a few miles above its confluence with the Nile, 12 m. S from Goz, in lat. 17° 42' N.
ACCADIA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Principato Ultra, near the source of the Carapella, 15 m. SW by W from Ascoli. Pop. 3,000.
ACCAR, one of the Kirrama group of islets to the westward of Loo-choo. - Beechey.
ACCARAH, or usually Accra, on the Gold-coast, in N lat. 5° 35', E long. 0° 5'.

ACCAWAYS, a tribe of Indians in British Guayana, located, to the number of about 700, on the Demerara river, and 1,500 on the Massaroony. They are an active and industrious, and yet a pugnacious race. "The travelling equipage of an Accaway Indian," says Mr. Hillhouse, "is calculated either to drive a bargain or to sack a village."—Geog. Journ., vol. ii.
ACCETTURA, a town of Naples, in the canton of San Mauro, prov. of Basilicata, 19 m. SSE from Acerenza.
ACCHA, or Agcha, a village in Peru, in the prov. of Chilques and Mospes, seated beneath an overhanging rock which seems constantly to threaten its destruction. Lat. 14° 12' S, 42 m. S of Cuzco.

ACCHAHUARI, an Indian v. in the Peruvian Sierra, on the road from Lima to Tarma, 8 m. E of Chicha, at an elevation of 13,056 ft. The pass of Antarangra or Pachaehaca lies to the E of this village.—Tschudi.
ACCHO. See ACRE.
ACCIAJOLO, a cape on the NW coast of Corsica, at the entrance of the gulf of San Fiorenzo.

ACCIAROLI, or Aciarolo (Punta Degli), a cape of Italy, on the Mediterranean coast, in the Neapolitan prov. of Principato Citra, to the NW of the mouth of the Alento; in N lat. 40° 10', E long. 14° 59′.
ACCIA-ROVINATA, a town of Corsica, in the arrond, of Bastia, once of importance, but now gone to decay.
ACCITES, a river of Colombia, in the prov. of Apure, running into the Orinoco.
ACCODA, a small Dutch settlement on the Gold coast, in the Ashantee country.
ACCOLORETTO, a town of Italy in the Statesof-the-Church, in the prov. of Spoleto, 7 m. SW from Todi.

ACCOMAC, a county of Virginia, U. S., on a peninsula separating Chesapeak bay from the Atlantic. It contains 480 sq. m., and a pop. in 1840 of 17,096.—The chief town of the same name is 193 m. E by N of Richmond.
ACCOMPONG TOWN, a town of Jamaica, in the parish of St. Elizabeth, 21 m. SE by S of Montago, near the sources of the Black river.
ACCONBURY. See ACONBURY.

ACCORAN, a town of Sudan, between the river Makammee and the country of the Fellatahs, 180 m. S of Kashna, in N lat. 10°. ACCRA; a river of Sudan, rising in the hills which separate the Fellatah country from Bobyra, and running westwards to the Makammee.

ACCRA, a small state, on the Gold coast of Africa, to the W of the Rio Volta, about 26 m. in length, and from 12 to 20 m. in breadth. It has the Agoona territory on the N, Inkran on the E, and Fantee on the W. The only river is the Seacoom, a small unnavigable stream. It is reckoned the most healthy situation of any on this coast; and carries on the most extensive trade both with Europeans and with the interior. The soil on the coast is light and sandy, and unfit for the production of sugar or corn; but is well-adapted for the growing of palm-nuts; yams, cotton, cassada, ACE and pulse, and a few miles inland it is excellent. The Danes have established a coffee-plantation. The people are a more quiet and orderly race than the Fantees and other neighbours; which is doubtless in a great measure to be imputed to the freer intercourse which they enjoy with Europeans. They have a language peculiar to themselves, and not understood by any other nation on the coast, which favours the idea that they came originally from the E. -The town of Accra lies about 75 m. to the E of Cape-coast. The landing at this place is bad; the beach is rocky, and the surf that breaks over it renders it dangerous, even for the canoes of the natives, when the sea-breeze sets in with any violence. Vessels discharge their cargoes in the open roadstead, 1 or 2 m. from shore. The British, Dutch, and Danes have settlements here. The fort of the latter, called Christiansburg, is on a large scale, and mounted with about 80 or 90 guns. The Ashantee traders frequent this place, and carry on a considerable trade, both at the British and Danish settlements. In 1840, 34 British and 21 foreign vessels visited A. The imports into British Accra, in 1840, amounted to from £65,000 to £70,000, and from £12,000 to £15,000 from America. They consist of cotton goods, iron, copper, lead, brass, pewter, beads, tobacco, and rum. The exports from British Accra, in 1840, amounted to from £85,000 to £95,000 to Europe; and from £15,000 to £20,000 to the United States. They consist of gold-dust, ivory, palm-oil, Guinea-grains, ground-nuts, and Indian corn. The palm-oil trade is rapidly increasing here, but the Danes have monopolized it to a great extent. The government of the British settlement is administered by the commandant of the fort, who receives a salary of £100 a-year from the government at Cape-coast Castle. The cost of the maintenance of the establishment is £500 a-year; the fort is in good repair.—Madden's Report:
ACCRA, or ARRA, a strong fortress in the Persian pashalik of Amadia, upon the Zâb river, 14 hours NNW of Erbile.—J. B. Fraser.

ACCRINGTON (New), a township in the co. of Lancaster, parish of Whalley, 54 m. E by S from Blackburn. Area 2,480 acres. Pop. 6,908. Since the year 1811 the pop. of this township has been trebled, by the extension of the cotton-thread manufacture, and the opening of new collieries.
ACCRINGTON (OLD), a township in the co. of Lancaster, 5½ m. E by S from Blackburn. Area 740 acres. Pop. 1811, chiefly employed in spinning and calico-printing.
ACCUM, a v. of Oldenburg, in the lordship of Kniphausen. Pop. 600.
ACCUMOLI, a town of Naples, in the canton of Amatrice, and prov. of Abruzzo Ultra; 17 m. NW from Aquila. Pop. 1,000.
ACEBEDO, a small town of Leon, near the sources of the Esla, 43 m. NNE of Leon, in Spain.
ACEGLIO, a market-town of Italy, on the Lago Maggiore, in the Milanese.
ACERE, a town of Italy, in the territory of Pavia, in the Milanese.

ACERENZA, or CIRENZA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicata, of which it was once the chief city; 16 m. NNE of Potenza, the present capital. It has a fine cathedral church, but the archbishop resides at Matera. It is supposed to be the Acheruntia of antiquity. Pop. 3,600.
ACERNO, a city of Naples, in the district of Salerno, and prov. of Principato Citra, on the site of the ancient Picentia; in a valley surrounded with mountains; 14 m. ENE from Salerno. Pop. 2,500.
ACERRA, a city of Naples, in the prov. of Lavoro, the see of a bishop, suffragan of Naples. It is in an unhealthy district; 8 m. NNE from Naples. Pop. 6,000
ACESINES. See CHENAB.
ACEYTE (SIERRAS DE), a range of high peaks rising inland near the gulf of Venezuela.
ACH. See AACH and ACK.

ACH, a river of Bavaria, in Ober-baiern, which passing Reil joins the Inn, on its l. bank, below Gars. -Also another river of Bavaria, in the circle of the Danube, which rising to the SW of Augsburg, and passing Mühlhausen, after running about 35 m. parallel with the Lech, forms a junction with that river on its r. bank, a few miles above its confluence with the Danube.
ACHA. See ACHEN.
ACHA, a mountain on the borders of New Mexico.
ACHACHACHE, a town of Bolivia, in the prov. of La Paz, on the N bank of a river which rises in the Cordillera de Acama, and runs into the E side of the Titicaca lake. Lat. 16° 3' S .- Pentland.
ACHAGUAS, an Indian tribe of New Granada, inhabiting the plains of the Cazanare and the Meta.

ACHAIA, or ACHEA, an ancient and a modern division of that part of Greece known as the Peloponnesus or Morea. The ancient A. extended along the S coast of the gulfs of Patras and of Corinth, from the Araxus promontory, the modern Cape Papa or Baba, on the W, to near the Sythas river in the territory of Sicyon on the E. On the S it was bounded by the mountains of Arcadia and Elis. Within these limits was contained a small but important territory whose inhabitants acted a distinguished part in the history of Greece. Early writers frequently apply the name Achaia to all Greece; and after the Roman conquest it was given to all the Peloponnesus with a large portion of Northern Greece: the whole of Greece being divided by a decree of the Roman senate into the two provinces of Macedonia, in which Thessaly and perhaps Acarnania was included, and Achaia. The modern A. has been made pretty nearly identical in boundaries and extent with that of the ancient prov. in its palmy days. In 1833 Achaia and Elis formed one of the ten nomarchies into which the kingdom of Greece was divided; and was subdivided into the four eparchies of Ægialia, Calavrita, Patras, and Elis. But in 1838, in the new and existing arrangement of territorial divisions, the two eparchies of Ægialia and Patras were formed into the department of Achaia, while the other two were erected into the department of Elis, thereby reducing A. to nearly its ancient limits. It is now bounded on the E by the department of Corinth; on the S by the departments of Cyllenia and Elis; on the W by Elis and the Ionian gulf. Its superficial area is 590 sq. m. Its chief towns are Patras, and Bosteitza or Vostitza, the ancient Ægium, both situated on the coast, at a distance of 25 m. Its pop. in 1840 was 33,190. A mountain-chain, the Panakaikon, beginning at Patras, divides into two principal branches, one of which stretches along the coast to Sicyon where it unites with a branch of the Cyllene, while the other runs southward dividing Elis from Achaia. Forests of oak and pine clothe these mountains. Along the coast extensive unhealthy marshes occur. The rivers of A. are all necessarily of a short and northern course, in summer nearly dry; in winter, from the melting of the snow on the Arcadian mountains, turbulent torrents rush into the gulf. Beginning at the E extremity of the territory there is the Akrata Acheenese differ considerably from the inhabitants or Sakratas, a shallow but rapid river. To the W of this river is the Bouraikos or Calavrita; and proceeding westwards we cross in succession the Selinos, the Phœnix, the Soria, the Selemnos, the Melikorikia, the Glaukos or Leuka, and the Kamenitza. The productions of the district are cotton, wool, silk, oil, cheese, wax, wine, currants, figs, almonds, citrons, corn, millet, and tobacco.—Dodwell, Sir W.
