ACHAIA PALAIS, a v. of Greece, in the above dep. 4 hours SW of Patras.
ACHAL, or ACHEL, a town in the prov. of Limbourg, 24 m. WNW of Roermunde.
ACHAL-KALAK. See AKHAL-KALAK.
ACHALZEK. See ARBALZIKHE.
ACHARKOI, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in Romelia, NW of Keshan, on a small stream which flows into the gulf of Enos. Lat. 40° 53' N.
ACHAR-SU. See AKHARSU.
ACHAS, or PUENTE ARCAS, a village of Spain, in the prov. of Galicia, 12 m. ESE of Vigo.
ACHASTIL LOCH, a small inlet on the coast of Knapdale, in Argyllshire.
ACHAU, a town of Austria, in the circle of the Lower Enns, near the fall of the Medlingerbachs into the Badnerbach. Pop. 646.

ACHEEN, or ACHEN, an independent state, forming the NW portion of the island of Sumatra, and the only part of that island that has yet risen to such importance as to become the subject of history. It comprises the whole of the northern extremity of the island; and extends in a SE direction about 60 m. from Cape Acheen to Cape Diamond on the N coast; and to Barus on the S, comprehending the chieftaincies of Pedir and Sinkel. Its pop. has been estimated at 2,000,000; but this is altogether conjecture. It is intersected by a range of mountains, which, beginning at Cape Acheen, runs towards the SE, where it joins the mountains of Sampanow. The principal bays are the Back bay to the E of Acheen, and the bay of Leaga. The principal rivers are the Sinkel, whose source is in the central range of mountains, and which has a SE course; the river Analabu, and the Acheen which falls into the sea near the capital, where it divides into several branches. The climate of A. is salubrious, the temperature being moderated by the sea-breeze. The soil is light but fertile; and marshes and woods are not so numerous or extensive in this quarter as in other parts of Sumatra. Its important productions are pepper, rice, cotton, sugar, coffee, gumbenzoin, and camphor. Cultivation is said to be more general than in other parts of the island. Cattle and a small species of horse are reared for exportation. Elephants, which are numerous, are employed as domestic animals. There are rich mines of gold and copper. Those which have been worked near Mucki, to the E of Soulou, are reported to be of great value. Though the commercial importance of A. has greatly declined within the last century, it still carries on a considerable trading intercourse with the Telinga coast of Hindostan. The principal articles of commerce are gold, pepper, betel-nut, sulphur, camphor, benzoin, and raw silk. Of gold alone 10,000 ounces were at one time annually exported from this state; while the pepper-crop of 1823 amounted to from 100,000 to 120,000 piculs, of 137½ tbs. each. The imports consist of Bengal opium, European manufactures, salt, and iron. In former times the sultan monopolized the principal trade of the country. The chief commercial towns are Acheen, Pedir, and Samalanga, on the N coast, and Analabu, Sinkel, and Taruman, on the S coast. The current money consists of a small piece of gold marked with Arabic characters, and named más: piasters and rupees are often met with, but payments are generally made with gold dust.—The of the other districts of Sumatra. In person they are taller, stouter, and of darker complexion; in disposition they are represented as more active and industrious, more sagacious and penetrating. The men have recently adopted the European jacket of blue or black cloth, and sometimes embroidered with fine gold thread; but the genuine Acheenese dress is a * collar; a pair of loose trousers reaching to the calf of the leg; and a kind of kilt, long enough to pass once or twice round the waist, and hang below the knees. A silken belt embraces the loins, concealing the upper edge of the kilt, and securing the trousers. The kris is always worn, and is often of great value. An Acheenese in full dress may remind one of the Highland uniform in the British army. [Voyage of the Potomac.] They are of Malay origin, with a mixture of Battas and Moors, and speak the Malay language; but in writing they use the Arabic characters. They profess the Mahommedan religion; their mosques and their priests are numerous; and they adhere with rigid exactness to the forms prescribed in the Koran. The government of Acheen resembles that of the greater number of the Malay nations. The monarchy is hereditary, but the succession is often transferred to a younger son when the elder is deemed less competent for the exercise of sovereignty. The reigning prince is called Tuankita, i. e. 'My master.' The power of the sovereign is limited by that of the rajahs or possessors of fiefs, who are always ready to resist the usurpations of the monarch; and in 1813 the whole district is said to have been in a state of anarchy and confusion. The revenues of the sultan are principally derived from the custom-dues on imports which usually amount to 12 or 15 per cent. The military and naval force of Acheen was at one period considerable. In 1573, the king or sultan of A., with a fleet which is described as filling the straits of Malacca, attacked three Portuguese frigates and destroyed them; and in 1615, one of his successors appeared before Malacca with an armament of 500 vessels and 6,000 men. Founderies of cannon are even said to have been established at A. The intercourse of England with A. dates from the year 1602, when Captain Lancaster visited this port of Sumatra. In a correspondence which subsequently took place betwixt the sultan of A. and king James, the former assumed the title of sultan of Sumatra.

ACHEEN, the capital of the above kingdom, is situated about 3 m. from the coast, on a river of the same name, which flows into the Indian ocean at the NE extremity of Sumatra. It stands in a valley, which is about 5 or 6 m. wide on the sea-coast, and is surrounded, in the form of an amphitheatre, by two lofty ranges of hills; and the town itself rises in the middle of a forest of cocoa-trees. According to Raper (3d. Ed.) it is situated in N lat. 5° 35', and E long. 95° 19'. It contains 8,000 houses, built in one story of bamboos and rough timber, thatched with cocoa-nut leaves, and raised on piles to the height of several feet from the ground as a security against the inundations to which the district is liable in the rainy season. The town is not fortified; but the king's palace—a rude and uncouth edifice which stood in the centre of it—was encompassed by strong walls, and a moat 25 ft. deep. Near the palace-gate were several pieces of brass ordnance of prodigious size. Some of these were made in Portugal; but two of them were sent to the sultan of Acheen by James I. of England, their date and founder's name being still visible upon them. A. has however for some years ceased to be the royal residence, the court having removed to Tulimasaway; but it is still "a considerable town, containing a great number of public edifices, but without any pretensions to magnificence. There appear to be no regular streets, each house being separate and surrounded with trees." [Voyage of the Potomac in 1832.] The A. river is 40 fathoms wide at the town, but is shallow at the bar; and during the dry monsoon vessels must anchor without. Besides the native Acheenese, this town is inhabited by Dutch, Danes, Portuguese, and Chinese, the latter of whom are most numerous, and carry on a consider- ACH able trade. The envirous are well-cultivated, and produce grain of all kinds in abundance. - Memoir of Sir T. S. Raffles.—Anderson's Mission to the East Coast of Sumatra, in 1822.—Marsden's History of Sumatra. - Voyage of the U.S. Frigate Potomac, in 1831-4.
ACHELOUS. See ASPROPOTAMOS.
ACHEM. See ACHEEN.

ACHEN, a river of Bavaria which rises in the Tyrol, at the northern foot of the Hinterstein, and flows N to the Chiemsee. This river is sometimes known as the Great Achen.—Also a stream rising in the Achensee, and flowing through the Achenthal, and the Tauern or mountain-ridge which divides Bavaria from the Tyrol, by the W side of the Blauberg, to the Isar which it joins on the r. bank.
ACHENAU, a small river of France, in the dep. of the Loire Inférieure, which conveys the waters of the Grandlieu lake to the Loire.
ACHENRAIN, a v. and chateau of the Tyrol, in the circle of the Lower Innthal, 3 m. NNW of Rattenberg.

ACHENSEE, a small and highly picturesque lake in the Tyrol on the summit-level of the road from Kreith to Schwatz, and washing the western base of the Gamsjoch. It is about 6 m. in length from N to S, but nowhere exceeds a mile in breadth. It is reported that this Alpine tarn was very severely affected during the great earthquake at Lisbon. It abounds in excellent trout. Lat. 47° 30' N.
ACHENTHAL, a v. and p. of the Tyrol, in the circle of the Lower Innthal, and to the N of the above lake, 18 m. N of Schwatz. Pop. 550.
ACHEPE BAY, or Ashpe Bay, a small port on the NE coast of the island of Cape Breton, to the SW of Cape North.

ACHER, a river of Baden, which issuing from the Mummel lake in the W of the Black Forest, and flowing in a NW course past Achern and Lichtenau, falls into the Rhine, on the r. bank, 3 m. NW of Lichtenau, after a course of 21 m.
ACHE'RES LE MARCHE', a town of France, in the arrond. of Pithiviers, and dep. of Loiret, 4 m. NNW of Neuville-aux-Bois. Pop. 1,330.

ACHERN, a district and town of Baden, in the circle of the Central Rhine, on the Achern, 10 m. SSW of Baden. Pop. of the district 17,349; of the town, 1,713. At the v. of Sassbach, in the neighbourhood of A., Marshal de Turenne was killed by a cannon-shot on the 26th July 1675, while superintending the erection of a battery against Montecuculli's lines.

ACHERON, a river of the Triphylian division of Elis, in Greece, rising on the N side of Mount Minthe or Vunúka, and flowing by a N and NNE course to the Alpheus, which it joins on the l. bank.—Also a river of Thesprotis in Epirus, which rises under a mountain called Lepé, near the head of the valley of Dervitzianà, and being joined by several rivulets, flows through a district called Laka, and the deep picturesque gorge of Suli, into the Paramithian plain at Glykys, where it loses itself in a marsh covered with willows, alders, and reeds, the remains of the ancient Acherusian lake, emerges again, and after receiving the Vava or Cocylus on the r., below the v. of Tcheuknider, flows into the Ionian sea at Glykys-Limen, or Porto-Phanari. Its prevailing course is SW, but its windings are numerous. Both these rivers—the latter especially—are intimately connected with Grecian fable; and the names Acheron and Acherusia are found in the topography of Pontus, Apulia, Campania, and the sacred territory of Memphis in Egypt. Perhaps the name originated in the latter district, and was carried by the initiated into other regions .-- Leake.—Bryant.—Hughes.
ACHEUX, a commune and v. of France, in the dep. of the Somme, arrond. of Doulens, 18 m. NE of Amiens. Pop. 940.
ACH-HISSAR. See AKHISSAR.
ACHICOURT, a commune and v. of France, in the Pas-de-Calais, arrond. of Arras. Pop. 1,310.

ACHILL, an island on the W coast of co. Mayo, Connaught. It is of a triangular form, and about 30 m. in circumference, but is so indented as to possess nearly 80 m. of coasts. Its area is 35,283 acres, and its pop. nearly 4,000. Its surface is mountainous and extremely wild, consisting of extensive bogs on the east, and lofty quartz hills on the other two sides. Its shores are more rocky and precipitous than those of any equal extent in Ireland. Its western extremity -known to mariners as Achill-head, in 10° 16' W long., and 53° 58' N lat.—is the most remarkable headland on the W coast of Ireland, and is elevated above sea-level 2,254 ft. It can be seen in clear weather at a seaward distance of 58 m., and forms therefore one of the most unerring guides to the mariner when he makes land crossing from America to Ireland.—Achill-sound, which is less than half-amile in mean breadth, separates the island from the peninsula of Achill or Coraan Achill, which is connected with the mainland by the narrow isthmus of p. 235. Molyrany, and extends about 6 m. westward, with a mean breadth of 4 m. It is a huge barren mass of conglomerate sandstone, peopled by about 600 mountaineers and bog-trotters. The pop. of the entire parish of Achill, which comprehends the island and peninsula now described, and the islet of Achillbega total area of 51,522 acres—was in 1831, 5,277; in 1841, 6,392, of whom only about 200 were Protestants. Houses in 1841, 1,158. The inhabitants of this district are rude and wretched to an extreme degree. Their cottages are frequently built without window or chimney, of round water-washed stones collected from the beach and laid upon each other without any other cement than the sea-sand, which is beat in between the two layers of stones forming the inner and outer walls, which are about 6 ft. in height. A few sticks placed across support the roof, the thatch of which is held on by ropes of hay laid over each other, and kept in their position by heavy stones fixed to their ends. A little dried turf and a ragged blanket usually form the bed; and the furniture—if there is any—is rudely fashioned out of any timber the cottier can command. During the spring, the entire population of several of the wretched little villages of Achill close their winter-dwellings, tie their infant children on their backs, and carrying with them some corn and potatoes, migrate to the hills, where they find fresh pasture for their cattle, and building bothies for themselves of sods and wattles, cultivate and sow with corn a few fertile spots in the neighbouring valleys; they then return to the shore and betake themselves to fishing, until the approach of autumn, when a migration similar to that of spring takes place for the purpose of reaping their little crops of corn.

ACHILLBEG, an island about 14 m. long, stretching out from the SE corner of Achill, and covering the S entrance of Achill-sound. The W end of the channel between it and Achill is dry at low water. Pop. 120. A vessel of 10 or 12 ft. draught may lie safe here. The bearing and distance from Achillbeg to Achill-head are NNW ½ W 12½ m.
ACHIM, a town of Hanover, in the duchy of Bremen, on the Weser, 10 m. ESE of Bremen. Pop. 960. It is the seat of a royal justiceship embracing 26 villages.
ACHKARN, a v. of Baden, in the bailiwick of Breisach, 3 m. NNE of Breisach. Pop. 430. Excellent wine is made here.

ACHONRY, a parish, bishopric, and village, in co. Sligo, Ireland. The p. is in length 8 m.; in breadth, 7; area 60,896 acres. Pop. in 1841, 17,986. Houses 3,186. The bishopric, now united to the diocese of Tuam, comprehends the greater part of Sligo, and a portion of Mayo. Pop. in 1831, 108,144. The cathedral is simply the parish-church of A.
